checks.a_analysis: I have examined the visible sections of the output (Slides 1, 2, 3, 20, 21) against the source PDF. All text content in these sections corresponds directly to the source. While Section 21 contains redundant elements (repeating the same sentence as both paragraphs and a list item), the information itself is present in the source and is not fabricated. The omitted sections are placeholders for token management and do not constitute fabrication.

The source document contains several URLs (https://lookbook.microsoft.com). These appear on slides that are part of the omitted content in the provided JSON snippet. There are no URLs or other contact information present in the visible sections of the JSON to compare. No fabricated contact information was found.

The output contains an explicit note that 16 sections were omitted for token budget reasons. The metadata correctly reflects a page count of 21 and word/image counts consistent with the full document. The visible sections (1, 2, 3, 20, 21) are present and correctly mapped to their respective pages. There is no evidence that pages were silently dropped; rather, they were intentionally collapsed for display in the audit interface.
The source document is a presentation containing text and images; it does not contain any tables. Therefore, this check is not applicable.

checks.check_name: Significant text omission within a section
checks.a_analysis: I compared the text within the visible sections (1, 2, 3, 20, 21) against the source. In every instance, the full text from the source page is captured in the output. For example, Slide 2 contains all four bulleted points and their descriptions as headings and paragraphs.

checks.check_name: Minor text differences beyond acceptable reformatting
checks.a_analysis: The text in the output faithfully represents the information in the source. Formatting differences (such as converting bullet points to headings or representing multi-line text with newlines) are acceptable and do not change the meaning. The duplication of text in Section 21 is a structural artifact but preserves the original information.

checks.a_analysis: The text content from Page 21 of the source document ('Most images you saw in this presentation, with the exception of the screen captures, were created by Microsoft Copilot') appears exactly once in the source as a single bullet point. However, in the output JSON Section 21, this content is duplicated. It is first rendered as two separate paragraphs (one for the lead-in text and one for 'Microsoft Copilot') and then immediately following, the entire sentence is repeated again as an item within an unordered list element.
checks.b_evidence_source: Most images you saw in this presentation, with the exception of the screen captures, were created by Microsoft Copilot
checks.b_evidence_output: The text appears twice: first split into two paragraphs ('Most images you saw in this presentation, with the exception of the screen captures, were created by' and 'Microsoft Copilot') and then again as a list item.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 21, Page 21, elements at index 0, 1, and 2.

checks.a_analysis: The heading hierarchy is consistent. The document title is used as the H1/Section Title, and major bulleted headers throughout the slides (e.g., on Pages 2, 3, 7, 8) are appropriately converted to H2 headings. There are no skipped levels or illogical nesting detected in the provided JSON structure.
The source document does not contain any tables.

The reading order generally follows the sequence of the slides in the source PDF. While there is redundant content in the final section, the information flow remains logical and consistent with the source's intended order.

On Page 21, the source contains a single bulleted list item. The output conversion is structurally flawed because it captures this content as two separate paragraphs and then again as a list item. This fragmentation and redundancy fail to faithfully preserve the simple list structure of the source.
checks.b_evidence_source: Most images you saw in this presentation, with the exception of the screen captures, were created by Microsoft Copilot (single bullet point)
checks.b_evidence_output: The content is split into two paragraph elements and then repeated in a list element.
checks.b_evidence_location: Section 21, Page 21, elements at index 0, 1, and 2.

checks.a_analysis: On Page 21, the text 'Microsoft Copilot' is formatted as a blue, underlined hyperlink in the source document. In the output JSON, while the underline property is captured, the link itself is missing (it is rendered as plain text within paragraphs/list items rather than a functional link element or a span with a URL property).
checks.b_evidence_output: The text 'Microsoft Copilot' is rendered with 'underline: true' but lacks an associated URL or link structure.

checks.a_analysis: The HTML output correctly translates the slide-based structure of the source PDF into a single-column, scrolling format. The visual organization follows the logical flow of the presentation, with headings, text, and images appearing in the same relative order as the original slides. This is an appropriate and functional layout for a converted document.
There are no tables present in the source PDF document to evaluate.

Several meaningful images are missing from the HTML output and are instead replaced by solid green placeholder blocks. Specifically, the M365 integration diagram on page 5 and the site screenshots on pages 16, 18, and 19 (which are the primary visual examples for 'The Perspective', 'The Landing', and 'Engineering Onboarding') are absent. These images are central to the presentation's content.
checks.b_evidence_source: Diagram with SharePoint logo and other M365 app icons on page 5; full-page site screenshots on pages 16, 18, and 19.
checks.b_evidence_output: Large green rectangular blocks appearing in place of images in the 'Integrated with Microsoft 365', 'The Perspective', 'The Landing', and 'Engineering Onboarding' sections.

checks.a_analysis: The text block describing the use of Microsoft Copilot for image generation is duplicated in the HTML output. In the source PDF, this sentence appears once on the final slide. In the HTML rendering, it appears twice as two identical, consecutive list items at the end of the document.

checks.b_evidence_output: "Most images you saw in this presentation, with the exception of the screen captures, were created by Microsoft Copilot" (appears twice in succession)
